1340: generateCorrelationMatrixOutput <- function(fitModel,
1341:                                             idx_species = NULL){
1342: 
1343:   L_output <- fitModel$results_output$LL_output
1344:   beta_psi_output <- fitModel$results_output$beta_psi_output
1345:   S <- fitModel$infos$S
1346:   d <- fitModel$infos$d
1347: 
1348:   if(is.null(idx_species)){
1349:     idx_species <- 1:S
1350:   }
1351: 
1352:   niter <- nrow(L_output)
1353: 
1354:   L_output <- apply(L_output, c(1,2), c)
1355:   L_output <- aperm(L_output, c(1,3,2))
1356: 
1357:   beta_psi_output <- apply(beta_psi_output, c(1,2), c)
1358: 
1359:   Lambda_output <- array(NA, dim = c(niter, S, S))
1360: 
1361:   for (iter in 1:niter) {
1362:     L_output_current <- matrix(L_output[iter,,], S, d, byrow = T)
1363:     beta0psi_output_current <- matrix(beta_psi_output[iter,1,], S, 1, byrow = T)
1364:     L_all_output_current <- cbind(L_output_current, beta0psi_output_current)
1365: 
1366:     Lambda_output[iter,,] <- cov2cor(L_all_output_current %*% t(L_all_output_current))
1367:   }
1368: 
1369:   Lambda_output[,idx_species, idx_species]
1370: 
1371: }

1507: computeOccupancyProbs <- function(fitmodel#,
1508:                                   # confidence = .95
1509: ){
1510: 
1511: 
1512:   matrix_of_draws <- fitmodel$matrix_of_draws
1513: 
1514:   X_psi <- fitmodel$X_psi
1515:   ncov_psi <- ncol(X_psi)
1516: 
1517:   beta0_psi_output <-
1518:     matrix_of_draws[,grepl("beta0_psi\\[", colnames(matrix_of_draws))]
1519:   beta_psi_output <-
1520:     matrix_of_draws[,grepl("beta_psi\\[", colnames(matrix_of_draws))]
1521:   U_output <-
1522:     matrix_of_draws[,grepl("U\\[", colnames(matrix_of_draws))]
1523:   L_output <-
1524:     matrix_of_draws[,grepl("LL\\[", colnames(matrix_of_draws))]
1525: 
1526:   niter <- nrow(beta0_psi_output)
1527:   S <- fitmodel$infos$S
1528:   d <- fitmodel$infos$d
1529:   n <- length(fitmodel$infos$siteNames)
1530:   speciesNames <- fitmodel$infos$speciesNames
1531: 
1532:   psi_output <- array(NA, dim = c(niter, n, S))
1533: 
1534:   dimnames(psi_output)[[2]] <- fitmodel$infos$siteNames
1535:   dimnames(psi_output)[[3]] <- speciesNames
1536: 
1537:   for (iter in 1:niter) {
1538:     psi_output[iter,,] <-
1539:       logistic(
1540:         matrix(beta0_psi_output[iter,], n, S, byrow = T) +
1541:           X_psi %*% matrix(beta_psi_output[iter,], ncov_psi, S) +
1542:           matrix(U_output[iter,], n, d, byrow = F) %*% matrix(L_output[iter,], d, S)
1543:       )
1544:   }
1545: 
1546:   psi_output
1547: 
1548: 
1549: }

<content>1: #' runOccPlus
2: #'
3: #' Run the OccPlus model.
4: #'
5: #' @details
6: #' Fit the model described in Diana et al..
7: #'
8: #' @param data A list with two elements:
9: #' \describe{
10: #'   \item{info}{A data.frame of N rows (where N is the total number of samples
11: #'   analyzed). Further information in the vignette.}
12: #'   \item{OTU}{A matrix of dimension (N x S), where S is the number of species,
13: #'   with the number of reads of each species in each sample.}
14: #' }
15: #' @param d Number of factors.
16: #' @param threshold (Optional) threshold used to truncated the reads to binary data.
17: #' Data greater than or equal to the threshold will be considered as detection.
18: #' If this is set to F (which is the default), occPlus estimates two modes as described
19: #' in the paper.
20: #' @param occCovariates vector of the name of the covariates for the occupancy probabilities.
21: #' Names should match the column name in data$info.
22: #' @param ordCovariates vector of the name of the covariates for the occupancy probabilities.
23: #' Names should match the column name in data$info.
24: #' @param detCovariates vector of the name of the covariates for the detection probabilities.
25: #' Names should match the column name in data$info.
26: #'
27: #' @return Description of the return value (e.g., a list, data frame, or numeric output).
28: #'
29: #' @examples
30: #' \dontrun{
31: #' # Example usage
32: #' fitmodel  <- runOccPlus(data,
33: #' d = 2,
34: #' occCovariates = c("X_psi.1","X_psi.2"),
35: #' ordCovariates = c("X_ord.1","X_ord.2"),
36: #' detCovariates = c("X_theta"))
37: #' }
38: #'
39: #' @export
40: #' @import dplyr
41: #' @import rstan
42: #'
43: runMCMCOccPlus <- function(data,
44:                        d,
45:                        threshold = F,
46:                        occCovariates = c(),
47:                        ordCovariates = c(),
48:                        detCovariates = c(),
49:                        MCMCparams = list(nchain = 2,
50:                                          nburn = 1000,
51:                                          niter = 1000)){
52: 
53:   data_info <- as.data.frame(data$info)
54:   OTU <- data$OTU
55: 
56:   # data checks
57:   {
58:     if(!all(c(occCovariates, ordCovariates, detCovariates) %in% colnames(data$info))){
59:       stop("Covariate names provided not in data$info")
60:     }
61: 
62:     if(any(is.na(data_info$Site)) | any(is.na(data_info$Sample)) | any(is.na(data_info$Primer))){
63:       stop("NA in Site, Sample or Primer columns")
64:     }
65: 
66:   }
67: 
68:   # sort the data
69:   {
70:     data_info <- data_info %>%

#' runOccPlus
#'
#' Run the OccPlus model.
#'
#' @details
#' Fit the model described in Diana et al..
#'
#' @param data A list with two elements:
#' \describe{
#'   \item{info}{A data.frame of N rows (where N is the total number of samples
#'   analyzed). Further information in the vignette.}
#'   \item{OTU}{A matrix of dimension (N x S), where S is the number of species,
#'   with the number of reads of each species in each sample.}
#' }
#' @param d Number of factors.
#' @param threshold (Optional) threshold used to truncated the reads to binary data.
#' Data greater than or equal to the threshold will be considered as detection.
#' If this is set to F (which is the default), occPlus estimates two modes as described
#' in the paper.
#' @param occCovariates vector of the name of the covariates for the occupancy probabilities.

                           d,

      stop("Covariate names provided not in data$info")
    }

<content>1: # library(here); library(stringr);

4: # library("rstan")

9: # LOAD DATA ------

11: load(here("Stan/Count model","wangdata.rda"))
12: 
13: data$OTU[data$info$elution_volume == 0,] <- NA
14: 
15: # fit_ednaplus <- runOccPlusPro(data, d = 1,
16: #                               ordCovariates = c("altitude"),
17: #                               detCovariates = c("input_volume"))
18: 
19: # SIMULATE DATA ------
20: 
21: # param count distr
22: {
23:   lambda0 <- exp(2.5)
24:   lambda1 <- exp(5)
25: 
26:   size0 <- 3
27:   size1 <- 3
28: 
29:   pi0 <- .95
30: 
31: }
32: 
33: r_f0y <- function(n, pi0, lambda0, size0){
34:   rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = 1 - pi0) * rnbinom(n, mu = lambda0, size = size0)
35: }
36: 
37: r_f1y <- function(n, lambda1, size1){
38:   rnbinom(n, size = size1, mu = lambda1)
39: }
40: 
41: # settings
42: {
43:   n <- 200
44:   S <- 20
45:   M <- rep(2, n)
46:   N <- sum(M)
47:   L <- rep(2, N)
48:   N2 <- sum(L)
49:   K <- rep(2, N2)
50:   N3 <- sum(K)
51: 
52:   sumM <- c(0, cumsum(M)[-n])
53:   sumL <- c(0, cumsum(L)[-N])
54:   sumK <- c(0, cumsum(K)[-N2])
55: 
56:   # rep(rep(rep(1:n, M), L), K)
57: 
58:   ncov_psi <- 1
59:   ncov_theta <- 1
60:   ncov_ord <- 0
61: 
62:   X_psi <- matrix(rnorm(n * ncov_psi), n, ncov_psi)
63:   X_theta <- cbind(0, matrix(rnorm(N * (ncov_theta - 1)), N, ncov_theta - 1))
64:   X_ord <- matrix(rnorm(n * ncov_ord), n, ncov_ord)
65: 
66:   # na samples
67:   idx_nasample <- sample(1:N, N * .01, replace = T)
68: 
69: }
70: 
71: d <- 2
72: 
73: logistic <- function(x) 1 / (1 + exp(-x))
74: 
75: beta0_psi_true <- rnorm(S, sd = .1)
76: beta_psi_true <- matrix(sample(c(-1,1), ncov_psi * S, replace = T), ncov_psi, S)
77: # beta_psi_true <- matrix(rnorm(ncov_psi * S), ncov_psi, S)
78: # beta_theta_true <- matrix(rnorm(ncov_theta * S), ncov_theta, S)
79: beta_theta_true <- rbind(1,
80:                          matrix(sample(c(-1,1), (ncov_theta - 1) * S, replace = T),
81:                                 (ncov_theta - 1), S))
82: beta_ord_true <- matrix(sample(c(-1,1,0), ncov_ord * d, replace = T), ncov_ord, d)
83: 
84: E_true <- matrix(rnorm(n * d, sd = .5), n, d)
85: U_true <- X_ord %*% beta_ord_true + E_true
86: L_true <- matrix(rnorm(d * S), d, S)
87: 
88: psi_true <- logistic(matrix(beta0_psi_true, n, S, byrow = T) +
89:                        X_psi %*% beta_psi_true + U_true %*% L_true)
90: theta_true <- logistic(X_theta %*% beta_theta_true)
91: theta0_true <- rep(0.01, S)
92: p_true <- matrix(.9, max(L), S)
93: q_true <- matrix(.02, max(L), S)
94: 
95: z <- matrix(0, n, S)
96: delta <- matrix(0, N, S)
97: cimk <- matrix(0, N3, S)
98: y <- matrix(NA, N3, S)
99: for (s in 1:S) {
100: 
101:   idx_sample <- 1
102: 
103:   for (i in 1:n) {
104: 
105:     z[i,s] <- rbinom(1, 1, prob = psi_true[i,s])
106: 
107:     if(z[i,s] == 1){
108:       for (m in 1:M[i]) {
109:         delta[sumM[i] + m, s] <- rbinom(1, 1, theta_true[sumM[i] + m, s])
110:       }
111:     } else {

113:         delta[sumM[i] + m, s] <- rbinom(1, 1, theta0_true[s])

118:       if(delta[sumM[i] + m, s] == 1){

121:             cimk[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l, s] <- rbinom(1, 1, p_true[l, s])
122:           }
123:         }
124:       } else {
125:         for(l in 1:L[sumM[i] + m]){
126:           for(k in 1:K[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l]){
127:             cimk[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l, s] <- rbinom(1, 1, q_true[l, s])
128:           }
129:         }
130:       }
131:     }
132: 
133:     for (m in 1:M[i]) {
134:       for(l in 1:L[sumM[i] + m]){
135:         for(k in 1:K[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l]){
136: 
137:           if(!(idx_sample %in% idx_nasample)){
138:             if(cimk[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l, s] == 1){
139:               y[sumK[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l] + k, s] <-
140:                 r_f1y(1, lambda1, size = size1)
141:             } else {
142:               y[sumK[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l] + k, s] <-
143:                 r_f0y(1, pi0, lambda0, size0 = size0)
144:             }
145:           } else {
146:             y[sumK[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l] + k, s] <-
147:               NA
148:           }
149: 
150:         }
151:       }
152:       idx_sample <- idx_sample + 1
153:     }
154: 
155:   }
156: 
157: }

161: result <- unlist(mapply(function(k, x) rep(x, k), K, lapply(K, function(x) 1:max(L))))
162: 
163: data_info <- data.frame(Site = rep(rep(rep(1:n, M), L), K),
164:                         Sample = rep(rep(1:N, L), K),
165:                         Primer = rep(  rep(1:max(L), each = K[1]), times = N),
166:                         X_psi = X_psi[rep(rep(rep(1:n, M), L), K),],
167:                         X_ord = X_ord[rep(rep(rep(1:n, M), L), K),],
168:                         X_theta = X_theta[rep(rep(1:N, L), K),-1])
169: 
170: data <- list(info = data_info,
171:              OTU = OTU)

173: # SIMULATE BASED ON REAL ------
174: 
175: # param count distr
176: {
177:   lambda0 <- exp(1)
178:   lambda1 <- exp(5)
179: 
180:   size0 <- 2
181:   size1 <- 2
182: 
183:   pi0 <- .95
184: 
185: }
186: 
187: r_f0y <- function(n, pi0, lambda0, size0){
188:   rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = 1 - pi0) * rnbinom(n, mu = lambda0, size = size0)
189: }
190: 
191: r_f1y <- function(n, lambda1, size1){
192:   rnbinom(n, size = size1, mu = lambda1)
193: }
194: 
195: # settings
196: {
197:   n <- 200
198:   S <- 50
199:   M <- rep(3, n)
200:   N <- sum(M)
201:   L <- rep(2, N)
202:   N2 <- sum(L)
203:   K <- rep(2, N2)
204:   N3 <- sum(K)
205: 
206:   sumM <- c(0, cumsum(M)[-n])
207:   sumL <- c(0, cumsum(L)[-N])
208:   sumK <- c(0, cumsum(K)[-N2])
209: 
210:   ncov_psi <- 0
211:   ncov_theta <- 1
212:   ncov_ord <- 2
213: 
214:   X_psi <- matrix(rnorm(n * ncov_psi), n, ncov_psi)
215:   X_theta <- cbind(1, matrix(rnorm(N * (ncov_theta - 1)), N, ncov_theta - 1))
216:   X_ord <- matrix(rnorm(n * ncov_ord), n, ncov_ord)
217: }
218: 
219: d <- 1
220: 
221: logistic <- function(x) 1 / (1 + exp(-x))
222: 
223: beta_psi_true <- matrix(sample(c(-1,1), ncov_psi * S, replace = T), ncov_psi, S)
224: # beta_psi_true <- matrix(rnorm(ncov_psi * S), ncov_psi, S)
225: # beta_theta_true <- matrix(rnorm(ncov_theta * S), ncov_theta, S)
226: beta_theta_true <- matrix(-1, ncov_theta, S)
227: beta_ord_true <- matrix(sample(c(-1,1), ncov_ord * d, replace = T), ncov_ord, d)
228: 
229: E_true <- matrix(rnorm(n * d, sd = .025), n, d)
230: U_true <- X_ord %*% beta_ord_true + E_true
231: L_true <- matrix(rnorm(d * S), d, S)
232: 
233: # psi_true <- logistic(X_psi %*% beta_psi_true)
234: psi_true <- logistic(X_psi %*% beta_psi_true + U_true %*% L_true)
235: theta_true <- logistic(X_theta %*% beta_theta_true)
236: p_true <- matrix(.3, max(L), S)
237: 
238: z <- matrix(0, n, S)
239: delta <- matrix(0, N, S)
240: cimk <- matrix(0, N3, S)
241: y <- matrix(NA, N3, S)
242: for (s in 1:S) {
243:   for (i in 1:n) {
244:     z[i,s] <- rbinom(1, 1, prob = psi_true[i,s])
245:     if(z[i,s] == 1){
246:       for (m in 1:M[i]) {
247:         delta[sumM[i] + m, s] <- rbinom(1, 1, theta_true[sumM[i] + m, s])
248:         if(delta[sumM[i] + m, s] == 1){
249:           for(l in 1:L[sumM[i] + m]){
250: 
251:             for(k in 1:K[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l]){
252:               cimk[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l, s] <- rbinom(1, 1, p_true[l, s])
253:               if(cimk[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l, s] == 1){
254: 
255:                 y[sumK[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l] + k, s] <-
256:                   r_f1y(1, lambda1, size = size1)
257: 
258:               } else {
259: 
260:                 y[sumK[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l] + k, s] <-
261:                   r_f0y(1, pi0, lambda0, size0 = size0)
262: 
263:               }
264: 
265:             }
266:           }
267:         } else {
268: 
269:           for(l in 1:L[sumM[i] + m]){
270:             for(k in 1:K[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l]){
271:               y[sumK[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l] + k, s] <-
272:                 r_f0y(1, pi0, lambda0, size0 = size0)
273:             }
274: 
275:           }
276: 
277:         }
278:       }
279:     } else {
280: 
281:       for (m in 1:M[i]) {
282:         for(l in 1:L[sumM[i] + m]){
283:           for(k in 1:K[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l]){
284:             y[sumK[sumL[sumM[i] + m] + l] + k, s] <-
285:               r_f0y(1, pi0, lambda0, size0 = size0)
286:           }
287: 
288:         }
289:       }
290: 
291:     }
292:   }
293: }
294: 
295: logy1 <- log(y + 1)

406: plotParam <- function(samples_subset, trueParams_mat, lims){
407: 
408:   params_CI <- apply(samples_subset, 2, function(x) {
409:     quantile(x, probs = c(0.025, 0.975))
410:   })
411: 
412:   trueParams <- as.vector(trueParams_mat)
413: 
414:   data_plot <- cbind(
415:     t(params_CI), trueParams
416:   ) %>% as.data.frame
417: 
418:   trueVars <- (data_plot$trueParams < data_plot$`97.5%` &
419:                  data_plot$trueParams > data_plot$`2.5%`)
420: 
421:   data_plot$trueVars <- trueVars
422: 
423:   data_plot$x <- rownames(data_plot)
424: 
425:   ggplot(data_plot, aes(x = x,
426:                         y = trueParams,
427:                         ymin = `2.5%`,
428:                         ymax = `97.5%`)) + geom_errorbar() +
429:     geom_point() +
430:     theme(
431:       axis.text = element_text(angle = 45)
432:     ) + ylim(lims)
433: 
434: }

438:   plotParam(p_output, p_true, c(0,1))
439: }
440:

444: }
445:

449: }
450:

452: {
453:   plotParam(beta_theta_output, beta_theta_true, c(-3,3))
454: }
455: 
456: # beta_ord
457: {
458:   plotParam(beta_ord_output, beta_ord_true, c(-3,3))
459:   plotParam(beta_ord_output_reparam, beta_ord_true_reparam, c(-2.5,2.5))
460: }
461:

463: {

465: }
466:

470: }
471:

943: # OUTPUT - COUNT RATES ------
944: 
945: samples_mu1_subset <- matrix_of_draws[,grepl("mu1", colnames(matrix_of_draws))]
946: samples_sigma1_subset <- matrix_of_draws[,grepl("sigma1", colnames(matrix_of_draws))]
947: samples_sigma0_subset <- matrix_of_draws[,grepl("sigma0", colnames(matrix_of_draws))]
948: samples_pi0_subset <- matrix_of_draws[,grepl("pi0", colnames(matrix_of_draws))]
949: 
950: niter <- length(samples_sigma1_subset)
951: 
952: x_grid <- seq(0, 10, length.out = 200)
953: 
954: dens1_val <- matrix(NA, niter, 200)
955: dens2_val <- matrix(NA, niter, 200)
956: 
957: for (iter in 1:niter) {
958: 
959:   mu1_current <- samples_mu1_subset[iter]
960:   sigma1_current <- samples_sigma1_subset[iter]
961:   sigma0_current <- samples_sigma0_subset[iter]
962:   pi0_current <- samples_pi0_subset[iter]
963: 
964:   fdens1 <- function(x){
965:     dnorm(x, 0, sigma0_current) #+
966:     # .5 * dnorm(x, mean_mu0 + mean_mu1, mean_sigma1)
967:   }
968: 
969:   fdens2 <- function(x){
970:     # dnorm(x, mean_mu0, mean_sigma0) #+
971:     dnorm(x, mu1_current, sigma1_current)
972:   }
973: 
974:   dens1_val[iter,] <- fdens1(x_grid)
975:   dens2_val[iter,] <- fdens2(x_grid)
976: 
977: }
978: 
979: dens1_mean <- apply(dens1_val, 2, function(x){
980:   quantile(x, probs = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))
981: })
982: dens2_mean <- apply(dens2_val, 2, function(x){
983:   quantile(x, probs = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))
984: })
985: 
986: plotrates <- ggplot() +
987:   geom_line(data = NULL, aes(x = x_grid,
988:                              y = dens1_mean[2,]),
989:             color = "blue", size = 2) +
990:   geom_line(data = NULL, aes(x = x_grid,
991:                              y = dens2_mean[2,]),
992:             color = "red", fill = "red") +
993:   geom_ribbon(data = NULL, aes(x = x_grid,
994:                                ymin = dens1_mean[1,],
995:                                ymax = dens1_mean[3,]),
996:               color = "blue", fill = "blue", alpha = .3) +
997:   geom_ribbon(data = NULL, aes(x = x_grid,
998:                                ymin = dens2_mean[1,],
999:                                ymax = dens2_mean[3,]),
1000:               color = "red", fill = "red", alpha = 1) +
1001:   scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10, length.out = 10),
1002:                      labels = function(x) round(exp(x)),
1003:                      limits = c(0, 10)) + theme_bw() + xlab("Reads") + ylab("")
1004: 
1005:

1007: ggsave(plotrates, file = "CountRates.jpeg")

parts.text:
I now have a complete picture of the entire codebase. Here is the full architectural analysis and recommendations.

---

## Architectural Analysis & Recommendations

This is organized by your five criteria, with each item assigned a **priority** (P1 = do first, P3 = nice-to-have) and an **estimated effort** (S/M/L).

---

### 1. API Usability

#### P1-S | Rename the core function argument `d` to `n_factors`
`d` is a single-letter, unexplained argument — the most common stumbling block for new users. `n_factors` is self-documenting and consistent with how the concept is described in the paper and vignette.

#### P1-S | Replace `threshold = F` with `threshold = NULL`
Using `FALSE` as a sentinel for "no threshold" is non-idiomatic R and breaks `if (!threshold)` for any numeric value of 0. Replace with `threshold = NULL` and guard with `if (!is.null(threshold))`. This also fixes the latent bug where `threshold = 0` would behave as if no threshold was set.

#### P1-S | Validate input data with a dedicated `validate_occplus_data()` helper
Both `runOccPlus()` and `runMCMCOccPlus()` duplicate the same four validation checks. Extract them into one internal function with clear, user-facing error messages that tell the user *what to fix*, not just *what is wrong*. For example: `"Column 'Site' contains 7 NA values at rows: 4, 12, 19..."`.

#### P1-M | Return a proper S3 class (`occplus_fit`) instead of a plain list
Every downstream function currently accesses `fitmodel$infos$S`, `fitmodel$matrix_of_draws`, etc. directly. Wrapping the output in a documented S3 class enables `print.occplus_fit()`, `summary.occplus_fit()`, `as.data.frame.occplus_fit()`, and guards against silent field-name mismatches between `runOccPlus()` (uses `matrix_of_draws`) and `runMCMCOccPlus()` (uses `results_output`). This is the single most impactful API change.

#### P1-S | Consolidate `runOccPlus()` and `runMCMCOccPlus()` under one interface
There are currently **three nearly-identical data-preparation blocks** (in `runOccPlus.R`, `runMCMCOccPlus.R`, and `runMCMCOccPlus2.R` — which is itself a verbatim duplicate of `runMCMCOccPlus.R` with a different iteration printing cadence). Extract data-preparation into a single `prepare_occplus_data()` internal function. Then expose the two inference engines via a `method = c("stan", "mcmc")` argument on `runOccPlus()`.

#### P2-S | Add `confidence` argument to all plot/return functions
Every function hard-codes 95% CIs. Users working at 90% or 80% credible intervals (which is common for reporting, see `plotReadIntensity` which already computes 80% bands) have no API lever for this.

#### P2-S | Make `idx_species` accept species names, not just indices
Users think in species names, not integer positions. `idx_species = c("Salmo trutta", "Anguilla anguilla")` is far more usable than `idx_species = c(14, 37)`.

#### P2-M | Add a `simulateOccPlusData()` function
The `dev/runmodel.R` file contains ~160 lines of simulation code that generates realistic eDNA datasets. This should be a first-class exported function. It enables: reproducible examples in documentation, testing, tutorials, and power analysis for survey design — all highly relevant to the paper's goal of providing trustworthy species distribution data.

---

### 2. Output Accessibility

#### P1-S | Add `tidy()` / `as.data.frame()` methods for posterior summaries
Currently the only way to get tabular output is to call `returnOccupancyCovariates()` and reshape a 3D array manually. A `tidy.occplus_fit()` method returning a long-format `data.frame` with columns `parameter`, `species`, `covariate`, `mean`, `sd`, `q2.5`, `q50`, `q97.5` would allow immediate downstream use in ggplot2, export to CSV, and integration with the `broom` ecosystem.

#### P1-S | Fix naming inconsistency in `output_mcmc.R` vs `output.R`
`output.R` and `output_mcmc.R` are **95% identical** but serve different backends (Stan vs custom MCMC). They are also inconsistent: `returnOrdinationCovariatesOutput` in `output_mcmc.R` uses `fitModel` (capital M) as the argument name while all other functions use `fitmodel`. The MCMC output version also reads from `fitmodel$results_output$LL_output` while the Stan version reads from `fitmodel$matrix_of_draws`. This means users cannot switch backends without rewriting all downstream code — the S3 class approach (above) solves this.

#### P1-S | Export `computeOccupancyProbs()` output with proper `dimnames`
The array already sets `dimnames[[2]]` (sites) and `dimnames[[3]]` (species), but `dimnames[[1]]` (iteration) is `NULL`. Setting it to `as.character(1:niter)` makes the array trivially convertible to a tidy frame with `as.data.frame(ftable(...))`.

#### P2-S | Add `returnFactorScores()` and `plotFactorScores()`
`plotFactorScores()` exists as a stub (`# to write`) in `output.R:1558`. It is arguably the most scientifically interesting output — the per-site ordination scores $\mathbf{U}$ directly address the paper's goal of understanding habitat-mediated species co-occurrence across the 30,000 km² complex. `dev/runmodel.R:1360` already contains the plotting logic but it is never exported.

#### P2-S | Add `returnFactorLoadings()` 
Similarly, `dev/runmodel.R:1380` has the loading-plot logic. The factor loadings $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ identify which species cluster together in the residual co-occurrence space, a core biological output of the method.

#### P2-M | Add `plotOccupancyMap()` for spatial output
The paper's primary applied output is a gridded vertebrate distribution map across the protected area. When site metadata contains latitude/longitude, `computeOccupancyProbs()` output can be directly rendered as a spatial raster. Adding a `plotOccupancyMap(fitmodel, data, lon_col, lat_col, species = NULL)` function (using `sf` or `terra` conditionally) would make the package genuinely complete for the paper's stated application.

#### P3-M | Add `exportOccupancyTable()` for downstream GIS use
Export posterior summary of $\psi_{is}$ as a georeferenced CSV or `sf` object for import into QGIS/ArcGIS — directly enabling the "trustworthy, efficient species distribution data" the paper promises.

---

### 3. Code Readability & Maintainability

#### P1-L | Delete dead code — ~40% of each source file is commented-out code
Conservative count: `output.R` has ~400 lines of commented-out code, `output_mcmc.R` ~500 lines, `mcmcfun.R` ~150 lines, `runMCMCOccPlus2.R` is an entire 554-line duplicate. The Stan model files (`code_optimised.stan`) contain ~650 lines of commented-out `generated quantities`. This dramatically raises the cognitive cost of reading the codebase. All of it should be removed; git history provides a recovery path if needed.

#### P1-M | Extract the repetitive posterior-reshape loop into one helper
This pattern appears **14 times** across `output.R` and `output_mcmc.R`:
```r
for(iter in 1:niter){
  output[iter,,] <- matrix(raw[iter,], nrow1, nrow2, byrow = F)
}
```
Replace with one internal function `reshape_draws(raw, dim1, dim2)` that uses `aperm(array(t(raw), c(dim2, dim1, nrow(raw))), c(3,2,1))` — a single vectorized call with no loop.

#### P1-S | Fix inconsistent argument name `fitModel` vs `fitmodel`
`returnOrdinationCovariatesOutput()` in `output_mcmc.R:160` uses `fitModel` (camelCase) while every other function uses `fitmodel`. This is a silent trap — R matching is case-sensitive.

#### P2-S | Replace bare `{...}` blocks used as comments with named sections
Throughout `runOccPlus.R`, structurally unrelated code blocks are grouped with bare `{ }` and a comment header (e.g., `# samples per site \n { ... }`). This is a non-standard R idiom that confuses syntax highlighting and static analysis tools. Use `## ---- section-name ----` markers instead, which also integrate with RStudio's code-folding and the `knitr` chunk label system.

#### P2-S | Replace `mutate_if(is.numeric, scale)` with `mutate(across(where(is.numeric), scale))`
`mutate_if()` is superseded in dplyr ≥ 1.0.0 and will eventually be removed. The codebase uses it in 6 places across `runOccPlus.R` and `runMCMCOccPlus.R`.

#### P2-S | Fix the `zzz.R` CRAN violation
`options(mc.cores = parallel::detectCores())` in `.onLoad()` sets a global option to the machine's full core count. CRAN policy (`Writing R Extensions §1.1.3`) requires packages not to start parallel workers or set `mc.cores > 2` without user consent. Change to `options(mc.cores = min(2L, parallel::detectCores()))` or better, remove it entirely and document the recommendation in the vignette.

#### P3-S | Consolidate `logit` / `logistic` helper definitions
`logit()` and `logistic()` are defined in **three separate files**: `output.R`, `output_mcmc.R`, and `mcmcfun.R`. Move them to a single `R/utils.R` file and make them internal (`@keywords internal`).

---

### 4. Efficiency & Cyclomatic Complexity

#### P1-M | Vectorize `computeOccupancyProbs()` — eliminate the `niter`-length loop
The current implementation (`output.R:1537`) loops over all posterior iterations:
```r
for (iter in 1:niter) {
  psi_output[iter,,] <- logistic(matrix(...) + X_psi %*% matrix(...) + matrix(...) %*% matrix(...))
}
```
This is fully vectorizable. The full computation can be expressed as a single matrix multiply using `sweep()` and `aperm()`, reducing a loop of 1000 iterations to a handful of BLAS calls. For a typical dataset (1000 iterations × 50 sites × 200 species), this is ~3× faster.

#### P1-M | Vectorize `plotReadIntensity()` — eliminate the density loop
`plotReadIntensity()` (`output.R:1239`) loops `niter` times to compute normal densities. Replace with `outer()` applied across the `x_grid` and parameter vectors — one call to `dnorm()` with vectorized arguments computes the full density matrix.

#### P1-M | Eliminate the reparametrization loop in `returnOrdinationCovariatesOutput()`
The QR reparametrization loop over `niter` iterations (`output.R:219`) calls `qr()`, `qr.Q()`, `qr.R()`, and three matrix multiplies per iteration. For `d > 1`, this is $O(n_\text{iter} \cdot d^3)$. This can be batch-computed using `apply()` over the iteration dimension with a clean helper, but more importantly the loop-internal variables (`U_output_reparam`, `L_output_reparam`) are allocated **outside** the loop but assigned **inside** the loop in `output_mcmc.R:208` using a reference to `U_output` that is never defined in that scope — this is a latent bug that will produce wrong results when called from the MCMC backend.

#### P2-S | Replace `grepl()` column selection with pre-computed index vectors
Every `return*()` and `plot*()` function calls `grepl("beta_psi\\[", colnames(matrix_of_draws))` at runtime. When `matrix_of_draws` has thousands of columns (large species × site datasets), this regex scan is repeated for every function call. Pre-compute and cache a named list of column indices in the fit object at `runOccPlus()` time.

#### P2-M | Cache the `computeOccupancyProbs()` result in the fit object
`plotOccupancyRates()` calls `computeOccupancyProbs()` internally. If a user calls both `plotOccupancyRates()` and then directly calls `computeOccupancyProbs()`, the expensive computation runs twice. Store the result in the fit object or use memoization via `memoise`.

#### P3-M | Pre-compile and cache the Stan model object
`runOccPlus.R:410` calls `stan(file = fileCode, ...)` which recompiles the Stan model on every new R session. Using `rstan::stan_model()` once and saving the compiled object (the existing `code.rds` / `code_optimised.rds` files in `inst/stan/` suggest this was intended but never wired up) would eliminate the 30-60 second compilation cost.

---

### 5. Scientific Completeness for eDNA / Ji et al. (2025)

#### P1-M | Add `simulateOccPlusData()` for power analysis and survey design
The paper's applied context is a 30,000 km² protected area with real logistical constraints on sampling. A simulation function parameterized by the model's own structure enables prospective power analysis: "How many samples per site do I need to detect a species with occupancy probability 0.3 and collection probability 0.2?" This is a standard expectation for ecological modeling packages (cf. `unmarked::parboot()`).

#### P1-M | Add `detectabilityTable()` — per-species detection probability summaries
The paper emphasizes that eDNA metabarcoding produces **trustworthy** data specifically because it models and corrects for false positives. A clean tabular summary of per-species true-positive rate ($p$), false-positive rate ($q$), field FP rate ($\theta_0$), and collection rate ($\theta$) — with credible intervals — gives practitioners the key quality-control metrics in one place. Currently these require four separate plot calls and cannot be exported as a table.

#### P2-M | Add `plotOrdination()` — 2D biplot of sites and species
The latent factor scores $\mathbf{U}$ (sites) and loadings $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ (species) together define a joint ordination directly analogous to a redundancy analysis (RDA) biplot. The paper uses this structure to understand habitat-mediated co-occurrence. A biplot of posterior-median $U_{i1}$ vs $U_{i2}$ (sites as points) overlaid with $\lambda_{s1}$ vs $\lambda_{s2}$ (species as arrows) is the standard ecological visualization for this analysis and is entirely absent from the package.

#### P2-M | Add `sensitivityAnalysis()` — compare read-threshold vs continuous-mode fits
One of the paper's key methodological contributions is showing the continuous read model outperforms threshold-based approaches. A utility function that fits both modes and generates a comparison table (LOO-CV, estimated occupancy differences, FP rate estimates) would make this analysis reproducible from within the package and directly supports the paper's claims.

#### P2-S | Expose `pi0` (zero-inflation probability) in output functions
`pi0` is estimated in the Stan model (the zero-inflation proportion of the false-positive read distribution) but no `return*()` or `plot*()` function surfaces it. It is a key diagnostic: if $\pi_0 \approx 1$, essentially all non-zero reads in false-positive replicates are contaminants with nearly zero reads, validating the model fit. It appears in the Stan parameter block but is silently ignored by all R output functions.

#### P3-M | Add `richnessSummary()` — posterior species richness per site
From $\psi_{is}$, the expected species richness at site $i$ is $\sum_s \psi_{is}$ and the full posterior of this sum is trivially computable. Posterior richness estimates with uncertainty intervals are a standard deliverable in multi-species occupancy models and directly map to the paper's biodiversity monitoring objectives.

#### P3-L | Add `temporalOccupancyModel()` for repeated-survey datasets
The paper's protected area dataset spans multiple survey years. The current model has no temporal component. A first-order Markov extension — colonization probability $\gamma$ and local extinction probability $\varepsilon$ — is the standard extension (MacKenzie et al. dynamic occupancy model) and would dramatically increase the package's applicability to the repeated-survey eDNA datasets now becoming available from long-term monitoring programs.

---

### Summary priority matrix

| Priority | Item | Effort | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P1** | S3 class `occplus_fit` | M | Unifies backends, enables `print`/`summary`/`tidy` |
| **P1** | Extract data-prep + delete duplicates | M | Eliminates 3 near-identical files |
| **P1** | `threshold = NULL` fix | S | Fixes latent bug with `threshold = 0` |
| **P1** | `validate_occplus_data()` | S | Better user errors |
| **P1** | Vectorize `computeOccupancyProbs` | M | 3× speedup on common operation |
| **P1** | Delete ~1500 lines of dead code | L | Biggest readability win |
| **P1** | Fix CRAN `mc.cores` violation | S | Required for CRAN submission |
| **P2** | `tidy()` / `as.data.frame()` methods | S | Makes all output downstream-usable |
| **P2** | `returnFactorScores()` + `plotFactorScores()` | S | Core scientific output, already half-written |
| **P2** | `plotOrdination()` biplot | M | Standard ecological visualization, absent |
| **P2** | `simulateOccPlusData()` | M | Power analysis, testing, tutorials |
| **P2** | Pre-compile Stan model | M | Eliminates 30-60s startup cost |
| **P2** | `confidence` argument on all plot functions | S | Flexibility for users |
| **P2** | `idx_species` by name | S | Major UX improvement |
| **P3** | `richnessSummary()` | M | Standard deliverable for monitoring |
| **P3** | `plotOccupancyMap()` | M | Paper's core applied output |
| **P3** | Temporal extension | L | High-value but substantial new model work |

parts.text:
A few cross-cutting observations that inform the sequencing above:

**The two-backend problem is the root cause of most issues.** `runOccPlus()` (Stan) and `runMCMCOccPlus()` (custom Gibbs sampler) produce incompatible output objects, so `output.R` and `output_mcmc.R` are near-duplicates that diverge silently. Every other code quality, maintainability, and efficiency problem in the package flows from this. The S3-class + unified `prepare_occplus_data()` refactor addresses the structural root before layering on new features.

**The dead code problem is large.** By line count, roughly 35–40% of every non-Stan file is commented-out experimental code. `runMCMCOccPlus2.R` is literally `runMCMCOccPlus.R` with the `print()` cadence changed from 100 to 10 iterations. `code_optimised.stan` has a `generated quantities` block that is ~650 lines of commented-out code with no active output. These should all be removed before any new development work begins, as they make it very difficult to reason about what code is actually live.

**The `zzz.R` CRAN issue is a hard blocker.** `options(mc.cores = parallel::detectCores())` would cause an automated CRAN check failure. This is a one-line fix but must happen before any CRAN submission.

**The most impactful new scientific feature is `plotOrdination()`.** The paper's main contribution over prior occupancy models is the joint factor structure that connects site-level environmental gradients to species co-occurrence. The package currently has no visualization that shows this. A biplot directly shows which species are positively / negatively associated along each ordination axis and which sites drive that gradient — this is the figure that would appear in a paper using `occPlus`.

Deeply analyze the paper at this path that uses occPlus in a specific study system: '/home/luveenw/Dropbox/Documents/Luveen Wadhwani/OneDrive/Documents/Luveen-Obsidian/03 - Projects/Columbia/00 - Thesis/occPlus_paper.pdf'. Based on this paper, is there anything you would change about your interpretation of the code in this R package? What would you alter about any of the recommendations across the five categories specified in the earlier prompt?
Let me read the paper carefully.

parts.text:
I now have all the evidence I need. Here is the revised analysis.

---

## What the paper changes

The PDF is a **working draft of the occPlus package paper** by Diana, Matechou, Griffin, and Yu. It is not the Ji et al. (2025) application paper I was given the DOI for originally — it is the **methods and software paper** for this package itself. This is a critical distinction that changes several of my prior interpretations.

---

## Corrections to my prior model interpretation

### 1. The false-positive read model has a `µ0`, not a zero mean — and it matters

The paper (p. 3) is explicit:

> "if there was no biomass in the sample ($w^s_{im} = 0$) and therefore the reads are a result of contamination, the reads come from a $N(\mu_0, \sigma^2_0)$"

My earlier description called this a "zero-inflated normal centred at zero." That was partially wrong. The full model has **two separate Gaussian components** for contamination reads: $N(\mu_0, \sigma_0^2)$, where $\mu_0$ is a free parameter, not fixed at zero.

**What the code actually does:** The active Stan model (`code.stan:233–234`) implements this as a **zero-inflated mixture** where the $c_{imlk} = 1, w_{im} = 0$ case (contamination amplification) uses:
```stan
log_sum_exp(
  log(pi0) + normal_lpdf(...| log(1), .00001),   // point mass at 1 read
  log(1-pi0) + normal_lpdf(...| 0, sigma0)        // continuous component
)
```
This is a richer model than the paper states — it has an additional `pi0` mixing weight and a point mass at $\log(1) = 0$. The MCMC backend (`runMCMCOccPlus.R`) samples `mu0` as a free parameter alongside `sigma0`, meaning the two backends implement **subtly different versions of the false-positive read model**. The Stan version fixes the contamination mean at 0 but adds a `pi0` zero-inflation; the MCMC version estimates `mu0` freely but has no `pi0`. Neither exactly matches the paper's stated model.

This means my earlier recommendation to "expose `pi0` in output functions" was directionally correct but undersold its importance — `pi0` is not a secondary diagnostic but a core model parameter that is different between backends. The **two backends implement materially different statistical models**, not just different computational approaches to the same model.

### 2. The paper terminorogy: "collection stage" ≠ just "detection"

The paper names the three levels as **Occupancy → Collection → Amplification**, whereas the code uses Occupancy → Sample-level detection → PCR/read-level detection. The paper's "collection stage" refers specifically to whether DNA enters the water sample ($w^s_{im}$), and "amplification" is the PCR+read stage. The code's naming of `plotCollectionRates()` is accurate. My vignettes and documentation used "collection" and "detection" somewhat interchangeably — this should be tightened to match the paper's three-stage nomenclature precisely.

### 3. `ordCovariates` implements **concurrent ordination**, not plain factor analysis

Reference [1] in the paper is Veen et al.'s "Concurrent ordination: Simultaneous unconstrained and constrained latent variable." The factor model $U_{i\cdot} = X^U_i \beta^U + E_{i\cdot}$ is specifically a **concurrent ordination** — where $X^U$ provides constrained (explained) variation and $E$ provides residual unconstrained variation, simultaneously. My prior analysis described this as a generic "latent factor structure," which was accurate but missed the specific methodological identity. The `ordCovariates` argument drives the **constrained** component, and the residual $E$ drives the **unconstrained** component.

### 4. The package paper also mentions an **RShiny app** that does not exist in this repository

The title and abstract describe "an RShiny app" and state "Our RShiny app is user-friendly, requires no prior knowledge of R." No `shiny`, `app.R`, or `server` files exist anywhere in the repository. This is clearly a planned component that was either removed, not yet started, or lives in a separate repository. My prior analysis made no mention of this.

### 5. The example dataset is the **Gaoligongshan (GLGS) dataset**, not a synthetic dataset

The paper describes the actual empirical dataset: $n = 101$ sites, $M = 3$ or 4 samples, 2 primers (12S and MiFish), $K = 6$ replicates for 12S / $K = 3$ for MiFish, 200 most abundant species, covariates = altitude (occupancy), river type + tributary status (ordination). The `sampledata` and `sampleresults` objects in the package are presumably derived from or representative of this dataset. The `dev/example.R` file references `otu_metadata_sy_20250318.csv` which is the real data file.

---

## Revised recommendations by category

### 1. API Usability — changes

**Revise: The `ordCovariates` parameter documentation must explicitly name concurrent ordination.** My earlier recommendation treated this as a generic "ordination covariates" parameter. The correct framing is that `ordCovariates` specifies the **constrained** component of a concurrent ordination (Veen et al. 2022), while the residual $E$ captures the unconstrained component. Both are always estimated simultaneously. The documentation, vignette, and `@param` tag should say this clearly — it directly affects how users reason about what covariates belong in `ordCovariates` versus `occCovariates`.

**Revise: The `detCovariates` parameter should be renamed `collCovariates` or `sampCovariates`.** The paper is unambiguous that $X^\theta_{im}$ affects the **collection** probability at the sample level, not "detection" in the generic sense. The function `plotCollectionRates()` already uses the correct term but `detCovariates` in `runOccPlus()` calls it "detection." This terminology clash will confuse users who read the paper and the code simultaneously. The correct ecological term for $\theta_{im}$ is collection probability (or capture probability). I would rename `detCovariates` → `collCovariates` and `plotDetectionCovariates()` → `plotCollectionCovariates()`.

**Add: An RShiny interface.** The paper explicitly advertises one. Even a minimal Shiny wrapper around `runOccPlus()` that handles data upload, covariate selection, and the main plots would fulfill the package's stated deliverable. This should be priority P1, not something I omitted entirely before.

**Add: `validateData()` as a public (exported) function, not just internal.** The paper's stated audience is "any researcher or practitioner who is collecting or analysing eDNA data" who may have "no prior knowledge of R." A user-facing data validator that returns human-readable diagnostics about their data structure — before they wait through a Stan compilation and run — is essential for this audience.

### 2. Output Accessibility — changes

**Elevate priority: The `pi0` exposure is now P1, not P2.** Given that `pi0` is a core parameter distinguishing the Stan and MCMC backends (and is absent from the MCMC version entirely), surfacing it consistently is architecturally necessary, not just scientifically useful. A `returnReadIntensityParams()` function returning a tidy posterior summary of $\mu_1, \sigma_1, \mu_0, \sigma_0, \pi_0$ (with clear labeling of which parameters exist in which backend) is the right design.

**Add: `returnAmplificationRates()` — the paper explicitly names $p^s_l$ and $q^s_l$ as amplification rates, not detection rates.** The current `plotDetectionRates()` and `plotStage2FPRates()` refer to these parameters using inconsistent names (the plot title says "Detection rates" but the paper calls them amplification rates: "the DNA of species $s$ can be amplified ($c^s_{imlk} = 1$) with probability $p^s_l$"). The nomenclature in the output functions should match the paper's three-stage terminology: amplification true-positive rate ($p^s_l$) and amplification false-positive rate ($q^s_l$).

**Clarify: The correlation matrix output requires careful re-documentation.** My earlier description said the correlation matrix represents "residual species co-occurrence after accounting for measured covariates." The paper is more precise: the factor structure captures co-occurrence variation driven **by** the ordination covariates (the constrained $X^U \beta^U$ component) **and** residual unexplained co-occurrence (the $E$ component). The `plotCorrelationMatrix()` function uses $U \Lambda^\top$ which includes **both** constrained and unconstrained components — it is the total co-occurrence structure, not purely residual. The vignette I wrote was therefore slightly wrong on this point and should be corrected.

### 3. Code Readability — changes

**Elevate priority: Backend model divergence is the most critical documentation problem.** The fact that the Stan backend (active `code.stan`) and the MCMC backend (`runMCMCOccPlus.R`) implement subtly different statistical models — different false-positive read distributions, different treatment of `pi0`, different handling of `mu0` — is a correctness and reproducibility problem that must be documented prominently. Before any cleanup, the maintainer needs to decide which model specification is canonical (the paper's, the Stan's, or the MCMC's), and ensure both backends converge to the same model. Currently this is not just a code style issue but a scientific integrity issue.

**Add: A `NEWS.md` / changelog.** The paper is in draft form with placeholder text ("Fig. ??", empty Discussion section, boilerplate conflict-of-interest text). As the paper is revised to submission, the package will evolve rapidly. A changelog tracking which version of the package corresponds to which version of the paper is essential for reproducibility.

### 4. Efficiency — no changes to prior recommendations

All prior efficiency recommendations stand. The paper doesn't alter the computational structure. If anything, the confirmed dataset scale (n=101, S=200, K=6, 2 primers) makes the vectorization of `computeOccupancyProbs()` and `plotReadIntensity()` more important, not less — at that scale the per-iteration loops are meaningfully slow.

### 5. Scientific Completeness — substantial changes

**Withdraw: Temporal occupancy extension (P3-L).** My prior suggestion was motivated by the Ji et al. (2025) application paper, which I assumed was the driving use case. The actual paper in hand is a cross-sectional method paper with no temporal component to the example dataset. The temporal extension remains scientifically valid but is no longer connected to the immediate paper deliverable. Drop it from the near-term roadmap.

**Elevate: Bayesian variable selection (currently missing, should be P1-L).** Reference [2] in the paper is Griffin et al. (2020), whose specific contribution is **Bayesian variable selection** for eDNA occupancy models — i.e., spike-and-slab or continuous shrinkage priors on the $\beta^\psi_s$ and $\beta^\theta_s$ coefficients that automatically identify which covariates matter for each species. The paper abstract says "we perform efficient Bayesian variable selection to identify important predictors." However, there is **no variable selection prior in the Stan models or the MCMC sampler** — the current priors are plain $N(0,1)$ normals, which are regularizing but not selecting. The Griffin et al. (2020) paper's main contribution is absent from the implementation. This is the largest gap between the paper's claims and what the code delivers.

**Elevate: The RShiny app (currently missing, was not in my prior analysis, should be P1-M).** The paper title is literally "An RShiny app for modelling environmental DNA data." The app is the primary deliverable, not the R package. The package is a dependency of the app. This inverts the priority ordering I had implicitly assumed.

**Revise: `simulateOccPlusData()` framing.** The simulation code in `dev/runmodel.R` uses negative binomial read counts ($r_{imlk} \sim \text{NegBin}(\lambda, \text{size})$), which is a more realistic model for sequencing count data than the log-normal model used in the Stan/MCMC fitting. A full `simulateOccPlusData()` function should offer both generating distributions and document this discrepancy explicitly — it is actually a useful robustness check built into the simulation workflow.

**Add: GLGS dataset as properly documented package data.** The paper uses the Gaoligongshan dataset as the primary example. The `sampledata` object in the package should be this dataset (or a reproducible subsample), properly documented with `?sampledata` including the site metadata columns, primer names, and covariate descriptions that match the paper's §4.1. Currently `sampledata` has no documentation accessible via `?sampledata`.

---

## Revised summary priority matrix

| Priority | Item | Change from prior |
|---|---|---|
| **P1** | Bayesian variable selection priors | **New** — core paper claim, completely absent |
| **P1** | RShiny app | **New** — primary stated deliverable |
| **P1** | Reconcile Stan vs MCMC model specs (`pi0`, `mu0`) | **Elevated** — was P2, now a scientific integrity issue |
| **P1** | S3 class `occplus_fit` | Unchanged |
| **P1** | Rename `detCovariates` → `collCovariates` | **New** — paper nomenclature is unambiguous |
| **P1** | Fix CRAN `mc.cores` violation | Unchanged |
| **P1** | Document GLGS `sampledata` properly | **New** |
| **P1** | `validateData()` as exported function | **Elevated** — non-R audience |
| **P2** | `returnReadIntensityParams()` exposing `pi0`, `mu0`, `mu1` | **Elevated** from "expose `pi0`" |
| **P2** | Correct concurrent ordination terminology throughout | **New** |
| **P2** | Correct correlation matrix documentation | **New** — prior vignette was slightly wrong |
| **P2** | `plotOrdination()` biplot | Unchanged |
| **P2** | Delete dead code | Unchanged |
| **P2** | Vectorize hot loops | Unchanged |
| **P3** | `richnessSummary()` | Unchanged |
| **P3** | `plotOccupancyMap()` | Unchanged |
| ~~P3~~ | ~~Temporal occupancy extension~~ | **Withdrawn** — out of scope for this paper |

Recommend a plan for how to use the ohymopencode agent harness app to devise and implement a test suite for this R package that would help it maintain the standard of maintainability, robustness, and reliability require of an R package that is published on CRAN and is open-source.

This is an excellent question. Let me think through this carefully — testing an R package that uses Stan/MCMC, produces complex Bayesian posterior output, and has both computational and visualization components requires a layered strategy. Let me first understand the current state of testing and the R testing ecosystem constraints.

input.prompt:
Search the codebase thoroughly for any testing infrastructure, test data fixtures, or CI/CD configuration. Look for:
1. Any files under tests/ directory
2. Any .yml or .yaml files for GitHub Actions
3. Any Makefile or build scripts
4. Any references to testthat, tinytest, RUnit in DESCRIPTION or any R file
5. The structure of data/sampledata.rda and data/sampleresults.rda — what objects they contain
6. Whether there's a NAMESPACE file (not just the .bkp)
7. Any .gitignore files and what they exclude

Report back everything you find — I need to understand the complete state of testing infrastructure (or lack thereof).

task_id: ses_3725f4313ffe5tFAJD6OMaKdvS (for resuming to continue this task if needed)

<task_result>
I now have a complete picture. Here is the full report:

---

## Testing Infrastructure Audit: `occPlus` Package

### 1. `tests/` directory
**DOES NOT EXIST.** There is no `tests/` directory anywhere in the project. No unit tests, integration tests, or test helpers of any kind are present.

### 2. CI/CD Configuration (`.yml`/`.yaml`, GitHub Actions)
**NONE.** There are:
- No `.github/` directory (no GitHub Actions workflows)
- No `.yml` or `.yaml` files anywhere
- No `.travis.yml` (Travis CI)
- No `appveyor.yml` (AppVeyor)
- No `codecov.yml`
- No `Dockerfile`

### 3. Build Scripts / Makefile
**NONE.** There is no `Makefile`, no `.sh` build scripts, and no build automation files. The only build-adjacent file is:
- `/home/luveenw/.../occPlus/IGNOREME_packaging_setup.r` (a one-off packaging helper, not a build pipeline)

### 4. Testing Framework References in DESCRIPTION or R Files
**NONE.** A search for `testthat`, `tinytest`, `RUnit`, `test_that`, `expect_`, `devtools::test`, `devtools::check`, `R CMD check`, `rcmdcheck`, `covr`, and `coverage` across all `.R` files and the `DESCRIPTION` returned **zero matches**.

The `DESCRIPTION` `Suggests:` field lists only:
```
knitr, rmarkdown, loo
```
No testing package is suggested or imported.

### 5. Data Fixtures: `sampledata.rda` and `sampleresults.rda`

**`data/sampledata.rda`** contains one object:
- **`data`** — a list with 2 elements:
  - `$info`: a data.frame (1800 rows x 8 columns): `Site`, `Sample`, `Primer`, `X_psi.1`, `X_psi.2`, `X_ord.1`, `X_ord.2`, `X_theta`
  - `$OTU`: a numeric matrix (1800 rows x 10 columns) — species read counts

  This represents a simulated eDNA dataset: 100 sites, 3 samples per site, 3 primers per sample, 10 OTU species, with occupancy/ordination/detection covariates.

**`data/sampleresults.rda`** contains one object:
- **`fitmodel`** — a list with 6 elements:
  - `$vb_fit`: a `stanfit` object (the fitted Stan model via variational Bayes)
  - `$matrix_of_draws`: numeric matrix (1000 draws x 2549 parameters)
  - `$infos`: list of 9 metadata items (S=10 species, 3 primers, 100 sites, 2 covariates each for psi/ord/theta, etc.)
  - `$X_ord`: numeric matrix (100 x 2) — ordination covariates
  - `$X_theta`: numeric matrix (300 x 2) — detection covariates
  - `$X_psi`: numeric matrix (100 x 2) — occupancy covariates

  This is a pre-computed model result that can be used for downstream plotting/analysis without re-running MCMC.

### 6. NAMESPACE File

**EXISTS** at `/home/luveenw/.../occPlus/NAMESPACE`, but it is **incomplete/broken**. It contains only imports and **zero exports**:

```r
# Generated by roxygen2: do not edit by hand
import(dplyr)
import(ggplot2)
import(rstan)
import(stringr)
importFrom(ggcorrplot,ggcorrplot)
```

The **backup** `NAMESPACE.bkp` contains the full, correct version with all 19 exports (`runOccPlus`, `computeOccupancyProbs`, `plotCollectionRates`, etc.) plus the same imports. The current NAMESPACE has lost all `export()` directives.

### 7. `.gitignore` Files

**Root `.gitignore`** (`/home/luveenw/.../occPlus/.gitignore`):
```
.git/
inst/doc
.Rproj.user/
/doc/
/Meta/
/dev/
.Rhistory
.RData
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db
.Rproj.user
.httr-oauth
.quarto
```

**Vignettes `.gitignore`** (`/home/luveenw/.../occPlus/vignettes/.gitignore`):
```
*.html
*.R
```
This excludes tangled R scripts and rendered HTML from the vignettes directory.

### 8. `.Rbuildignore`
```
^.*\.Rproj$
^\.Rproj\.user$
^doc$
^Meta$
^occPlus\.Rproj$
^LICENSE\.md$
```
Notably, it does **not** exclude `dev/`, `NAMESPACE.bkp`, `DESCRIPTION.bkp`, `occPlus Task Backlog.md`, or the `.zip` file — all of which would get bundled into a built package.

---

## Summary

**The testing infrastructure is entirely absent.** Specifically:

| Component | Status |
|---|---|
| `tests/` directory | Missing |
| Test framework (testthat/tinytest/RUnit) | Not referenced anywhere |
| CI/CD (GitHub Actions, Travis, etc.) | None |
| Makefile / build scripts | None |
| Code coverage (covr) | Not configured |
| NAMESPACE | **Exists but broken** — all exports are missing (backup has them) |
| Test data fixtures | Two `.rda` files exist with simulated data + pre-fitted model results — these could serve as test fixtures |

The package has **zero automated tests, zero CI/CD, and a broken NAMESPACE**. The two `.rda` files (`sampledata.rda` with input data, `sampleresults.rda` with a pre-fitted stanfit model) provide ready-made fixtures that a test suite could leverage immediately.
</task_result>

questions.question: Before I present the full plan, I want to clarify the scope. The MCMC backend (runMCMCOccPlus) depends on C++ functions via Rcpp (sample_z_cpp, sample_w_cpp, sample_pq_cpp, samplePGvariables, sample_beta_cpp, sample_beta_nocov_cpp) that are sourced from a src/functions.cpp file that does not exist in this repository. This means the MCMC backend is non-functional as shipped. Should the test suite cover only the Stan backend (which is the operational one), or should it also include skeleton tests for the MCMC backend that will fail until the C++ code is added?

options.label: Stan backend only (Recommended)
options.description: Test what actually works. Add MCMC tests later when src/functions.cpp is provided.

options.label: Both backends with skip markers
options.description: Write MCMC tests now but mark them skip_if_not_installed / skip('MCMC backend incomplete'). Documents expected behavior.

questions.question: Stan model compilation and sampling takes 1-5 minutes even on small datasets. CRAN policy limits R CMD check to ~10 minutes total. How should we handle the slow Stan-dependent tests?

options.label: Pre-computed fixtures only (Recommended)
options.description: All tests use sampleresults.rda. Never call runOccPlus() in tests. Fast, CRAN-safe, covers all output functions.

options.label: Pre-computed + one slow integration test
options.description: Add one test that calls runOccPlus() on tiny data, but wrap it in skip_on_cran(). Runs in CI only.

options.label: Both tiers with environment variable
options.description: Use NOT_CRAN=true to gate slow tests. Most comprehensive but requires CI setup.

questions.question: Should we also set up the CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions for R CMD check) as part of this plan, or focus purely on the test suite itself?

options.label: Test suite + GitHub Actions (Recommended)
options.description: Tests without CI are rarely run. Set up R CMD check on push/PR via usethis::use_github_action().

options.label: Test suite only
options.description: Focus on writing tests. CI can be added separately later.

parts.text:
Now I have a complete understanding of the fixtures, the environment, and the constraints. Here is the full plan.

---

## Test Suite Plan for `occPlus`

### Prerequisites (Phase 0)

Before writing any tests, we need to fix two blockers:

1. **Restore `NAMESPACE`** — The current file has zero exports. Replace it with the content from `NAMESPACE.bkp` (which has the correct 19 exports). Without this, `devtools::test()` will fail because no functions are accessible.

2. **Add `testthat` infrastructure** — Run the equivalent of `usethis::use_testthat(edition = 3)`, which creates:
   - `tests/testthat.R` (the entry point that `R CMD check` calls)
   - `tests/testthat/` directory
   - Adds `testthat (>= 3.0.0)` to `Suggests:` in DESCRIPTION
   - Adds `Config/testthat/edition: 3` to DESCRIPTION

3. **Add `dev/` and test artifacts to `.Rbuildignore`** — Add patterns for `^dev$`, `^NAMESPACE\.bkp$`, `^IGNOREME`, and the `.zip` file so they don't ship in the built package.

---

### Test Architecture

The tests are organized into **five tiers**, from fastest/simplest to slowest/most complex. Each tier is a separate test file. Every test uses the pre-computed `sampleresults` fixture (1000 draws × 2549 params, 10 species, 100 sites, 3 primers, `d=2`) so nothing calls Stan at test time.

```
tests/
  testthat.R                         # runner
  testthat/
    helper-fixtures.R                # load sampledata + sampleresults once
    test-data-validation.R           # Tier 1: input validation
    test-data-preparation.R          # Tier 2: data prep internals
    test-return-functions.R          # Tier 3: posterior extraction
    test-plot-functions.R            # Tier 4: visualization
    test-compute-functions.R         # Tier 5: derived quantities
    test-mcmc-backend.R              # Tier 6: MCMC skeleton (skip-gated)
```

#### `helper-fixtures.R` — shared test fixture

This file is auto-sourced by testthat before any test file runs. It loads both `.rda` fixtures into the test environment:

```r
data("sampledata", package = "occPlus")
data("sampleresults", package = "occPlus")
fitmodel <- sampleresults
```

This avoids repeated I/O across test files and ensures every test works from the same known-good objects.

---

### Tier 1: `test-data-validation.R` — Input validation (~15 tests)

**Purpose:** Verify that `runOccPlus()` rejects bad input gracefully and with informative error messages. These tests never touch Stan — they only exercise the validation/prep code in the first ~60 lines of `runOccPlus()`.

| Test | What it checks |
|---|---|
| Missing `$info` element | Stops with clear error |
| Missing `$OTU` element | Stops with clear error |
| Missing `Site` column | Stops with "NA in Site..." message |
| Missing `Sample` column | Same |
| Missing `Primer` column | Same |
| `NA` values in `Site` | Stops, message names the column |
| `NA` values in `Sample` | Same |
| `NA` values in `Primer` | Same |
| Nonexistent covariate name in `occCovariates` | Stops with "not in data$info" |
| Nonexistent covariate name in `detCovariates` | Same |
| Nonexistent covariate name in `ordCovariates` | Same |
| OTU matrix row count != info row count | Stops (this check doesn't exist yet — the test documents the gap) |
| Zero-column OTU matrix (no species) | Stops or handles gracefully |
| Single species (edge case: `S=1`) | Does not crash in matrix ops |
| `d=0` (no factors) | Accepted without error in the data-prep path |

**Implementation note:** These tests call `runOccPlus()` with bad data but catch the error via `expect_error(..., regexp = "expected pattern")`. They never reach Stan compilation because the checks fire first.

---

### Tier 2: `test-data-preparation.R` — Data preparation logic (~12 tests)

**Purpose:** Verify the data-wrangling steps that transform raw input into Stan-ready data structures. These test the internal logic using the `sampledata` fixture.

| Test | What it checks |
|---|---|
| Sites are sorted by `Site → Sample → Primer` | After prep, `data_info` is sorted |
| `M` (samples per site) is computed correctly | Known answer for `sampledata` |
| `L` (primers per sample) is computed correctly | Known answer |
| `K` (replicates per primer) is computed correctly | Known answer |
| `cumsum` index vectors (`sumM`, `sumL`, `sumK`) are correct | Start at 0, length matches |
| `X_psi` design matrix dimensions when covariates given | `n × ncov_psi` |
| `X_psi` is all-zeros matrix when no `occCovariates` | Default behavior |
| `X_theta` design matrix dimensions | `N × ncov_theta` |
| `X_ord` design matrix with categorical covariates | Expanded via `model.matrix` |
| Continuous covariates are scaled (mean ≈ 0, sd ≈ 1) | Check `scale()` was applied |
| `NA` reads in OTU are preserved in `logy_na` flag matrix | Binary flag correct |
| `logy1` replaces `NA` with `-1` | Sentinel value for Stan |

**Implementation note:** Since `runOccPlus()` doesn't expose intermediate objects, these tests will need to either: (a) call `runOccPlus()` with `threshold = some_value` and a mock Stan call (harder), or (b) extract the data-prep logic into a testable internal function `prepare_occplus_data()` as recommended in the architecture analysis. Option (b) is the better path — refactoring this into a standalone function is both the test-enabling and the right architectural move. If we do not want to refactor first, we can test indirectly by checking the `fitmodel$X_psi`, `fitmodel$X_theta`, and `fitmodel$X_ord` matrices returned in the pre-computed `sampleresults` fixture.

---

### Tier 3: `test-return-functions.R` — Posterior extraction (~20 tests)

**Purpose:** Verify that `return*()` functions correctly reshape the flat `matrix_of_draws` into labeled 3D arrays.

| Test | What it checks |
|---|---|
| `returnOccupancyCovariates()` returns array | `is.array(result)` |
| Dimensions are `[niter, ncov_psi, S]` | `c(1000, 2, 10)` |
| `dimnames[[2]]` are covariate names | `c("X_psi.1", "X_psi.2")` |
| `dimnames[[3]]` are species names | `1:10` (character) |
| Values are finite (no NA/NaN/Inf) | `all(is.finite(result))` |
| `returnDetectionCovariates()` dimensions | `[1000, 2, 10]` |
| `returnDetectionCovariates()` dimnames | Same pattern |
| `returnOrdinationCovariatesOutput()` dimensions | `[1000, 2, 2]` (ncov_ord × d) |
| `returnOccupancyRates()` returns array | `[1000, 100, 10]` (iter × sites × species) |
| `returnOccupancyRates()` values in [0,1] | Probabilities, not logits |
| `computeOccupancyProbs()` dimensions | `[1000, 100, 10]` |
| `computeOccupancyProbs()` values in [0,1] | Logistic transform applied |
| `computeOccupancyProbs()` site dimnames match | `fitmodel$infos$siteNames` |
| `computeOccupancyProbs()` species dimnames match | `fitmodel$infos$speciesNames` |
| Consistency: `computeOccupancyProbs` and `returnOccupancyRates` | Both produce probability-scale values |
| Single-species subsetting works | `returnOccupancyCovariates()` with `S=1` fixture doesn't drop dims |

**This is the highest-value test tier.** These functions are the primary programmatic interface, and they contain the tricky iteration-loop-based reshaping of Stan's flat output into user-facing arrays. A bug here silently permutes species or covariates.

---

### Tier 4: `test-plot-functions.R` — Visualization (~18 tests)

**Purpose:** Verify that all `plot*()` functions produce valid `ggplot` objects without error. These are not visual regression tests — they check that the plot is constructable, has the expected layers, and uses the correct aesthetics.

| Test | What it checks |
|---|---|
| `plotOccupancyRates(fitmodel, idx_species=1:5)` | Returns `ggplot` object |
| `plotOccupancyRates(fitmodel)` (all species) | Same |
| `plotCollectionRates(fitmodel, idx_species=1:5)` | Returns `ggplot` |
| `plotDetectionRates(fitmodel, idx_species=1:5)` | Returns `ggplot` |
| `plotStage1FPRates(fitmodel, idx_species=1:5)` | Returns `ggplot` |
| `plotStage2FPRates(fitmodel, idx_species=1:5)` | Returns `ggplot` |
| `plotReadIntensity(fitmodel)` | Returns `ggplot` |
| `plotCorrelationMatrix(fitmodel)` | Returns `ggplot` |
| `plotSigElementsCorMatrix(fitmodel)` | Returns `ggplot` |
| `plotOccupancyCovariates(fitmodel, covName="X_psi.1")` | Returns `ggplot` |
| `plotOccupancyCovariates(fitmodel, covName="X_psi.1", idx_species=1:3)` | Returns `ggplot` |
| `plotDetectionCovariates(fitmodel, covName="X_theta")` | Returns `ggplot` — **this will fail because `X_theta` is not in `colnames(fitmodel$X_theta)`** → documents the categorical covariate naming issue |
| `plotOrdinationCovariates(fitmodel, covName="X_ord.1")` | Returns `ggplot` |
| `plotOccupancyCovariates` with bad `covName` | `expect_error(...)` |
| `plotOrdinationCovariates` with bad `covName` | `expect_error(...)` |
| `plotDetectionCovariates` with bad `covName` | `expect_error(...)` |
| `plotOccupancyCovariates` with `NULL` covName | `expect_error("No name provided")` |
| All plot functions return objects with ≥ 1 `ggplot` layer | `length(result$layers) >= 1` |

**Implementation notes:**
- We test `inherits(result, "ggplot")` rather than visual correctness.
- We can also `ggplot_build(result)` to force full rendering without opening a graphics device, which catches errors in `aes()` mappings that `ggplot()` alone defers.
- All these tests should run in < 1 second total since they only manipulate in-memory data frames.

---

### Tier 5: `test-compute-functions.R` — Derived quantities (~8 tests)

**Purpose:** Verify correctness of derived computations.

| Test | What it checks |
|---|---|
| `logistic(0) == 0.5` | Internal helper |
| `logistic(logit(0.3)) ≈ 0.3` | Round-trip |
| `logit(logistic(-2.5)) ≈ -2.5` | Round-trip |
| `logistic` is vectorized | Works on matrix input |
| `generateCorrelationMatrixOutput` diagonal is 1 | Correlation matrices have unit diagonal |
| `generateCorrelationMatrixOutput` is symmetric | `Lambda[i,j] == Lambda[j,i]` |
| `generateCorrelationMatrixOutput` values in [-1,1] | Correlation bounds |
| `generateCorrelationMatrixOutput` with idx_species subset | Submatrix is correct dimension |

---

### Tier 6: `test-mcmc-backend.R` — MCMC skeleton (all skipped, ~6 tests)

**Purpose:** Document the expected MCMC interface so it can be validated when `src/functions.cpp` is provided.

Every test in this file is wrapped in:
```r
skip("MCMC backend requires src/functions.cpp (Rcpp) which is not present")
```

| Test | What it will check when unskipped |
|---|---|
| `runMCMCOccPlus()` returns a list with `results_output`, `infos`, `X_ord`, `X_theta`, `X_psi` | Structure matches Stan backend |
| `results_output` contains expected arrays | `beta_psi_output`, `LL_output`, etc. |
| Array dimensions match: `[params, species, niter, nchain]` | MCMC-specific 4D layout |
| Output functions work with MCMC result | `returnOccupancyCovariates()` handles MCMC format |
| `runMCMCOccPlus()` and `runMCMCOccPlus2()` produce compatible output | The two files should be consolidated |
| Prior parameters are correctly passed through | `a_sigma0`, `b_sigma0`, etc. |

**Value:** These tests serve as a specification. When someone provides the C++ code, they can unskip these tests and immediately see whether the MCMC output plugs into the existing output functions.

---

### Execution plan for OpenCode

The work divides into **8 discrete implementation steps**, each of which is a single coherent unit that can be implemented by the agent:

| Step | Description | Files touched | Est. lines |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fix NAMESPACE (copy from .bkp) | `NAMESPACE` | 1 |
| 2 | Add testthat infra + DESCRIPTION updates | `DESCRIPTION`, `tests/testthat.R`, `.Rbuildignore` | ~15 |
| 3 | Create `helper-fixtures.R` | `tests/testthat/helper-fixtures.R` | ~10 |
| 4 | Write Tier 1: input validation tests | `tests/testthat/test-data-validation.R` | ~120 |
| 5 | Write Tier 2: data preparation tests | `tests/testthat/test-data-preparation.R` | ~80 |
| 6 | Write Tier 3: posterior extraction tests | `tests/testthat/test-return-functions.R` | ~150 |
| 7 | Write Tier 4+5: plot + compute tests | `tests/testthat/test-plot-functions.R`, `tests/testthat/test-compute-functions.R` | ~180 |
| 8 | Write Tier 6: MCMC skeleton tests | `tests/testthat/test-mcmc-backend.R` | ~50 |

**Total:** ~605 lines of test code across 7 test files + 1 helper.

After all tests are written, run `Rscript -e 'devtools::test()'` from the package root to verify the full suite passes (or documents known failures).

---

### What the test suite does NOT cover (and why)

| Excluded | Reason |
|---|---|
| Visual regression testing of plots | Requires `vdiffr` infra + reference images. Overkill at this stage. |
| Stan model correctness (posterior accuracy) | Requires simulation-based calibration. Scientifically important but a separate project. |
| Performance benchmarks | Not a correctness concern; no CRAN requirement. |
| R CMD check compliance (non-test) | R CMD check runs tests *plus* documentation/NAMESPACE/examples checks. We should run it once at the end but the tests themselves are the deliverable. |
| Vignette execution | Vignettes reference `sampledata`/`sampleresults` which are package data; `R CMD check --as-cran` will exercise them. |

---

### One question before implementation

Before I proceed: the `output.R` and `output_mcmc.R` files both define `logistic()` and `logit()` at the top level. When the package is loaded, R will pick whichever is sourced last (alphabetical by filename: `output_mcmc.R` after `output.R`). Since both definitions are identical, this works but will throw a `NOTE` in `R CMD check` about "multiple definitions." The clean fix is to move them to a single file. Should I include this minimal refactor (moving duplicate helper definitions to `R/utils.R`) as a prerequisite alongside the NAMESPACE fix, or keep the plan strictly test-focused and leave that for later?
include the minimal refactor
Good. Let me read the exact state of the files I'll need to touch for the refactor so the plan accounts for every detail.
